global_disclaimer: I am not a medical professional. This chatbot provides educational information only and does not replace professional medical advice. If emotional symptoms are severe, persistent, or interfering with daily life, speak with a trusted adult or healthcare provider.

qa.question: What is PMS?
qa.answer: PMS has a combination of both physical and emotional symptoms that typically occur after ovulation and before the start of the next menstrual period. It occurs because estrogen and progesterone levels intensely decrease when not pregnant.

qa.question: Who gets PMS?
qa.answer: 3 in 4 women experience PMS symptoms at some point in their lifetime.

qa.question: When can PMS happen more often?
qa.answer: PMS may occur more often in people with high levels of stress, a family history of depression, or a personal history of postpartum depression or depression.

qa.question: What are the symptoms of PMS? (Emotional/mental)
qa.answer: Symptoms may include irritability or hostile behavior, exhaustion, sleep problems (too much or too little sleep), appetite changes, food cravings, trouble concentrating or remembering things, tension or anxiety, depression or prolonged sadness, mood swings, and less interest in sex.

qa.question: How does PMS affect other health problems? (Emotional/mental)
qa.answer: Depression and anxiety disorders are the most common conditions that overlap with PMS. Symptoms typically worsen before or during this period.

qa.question: How does the menstrual cycle affect my emotions and energy level?
qa.answer: During the first half of the cycle (week 1–2 after the period starts), people may experience higher energy levels, better memory, and higher pain tolerance. During the second half of the cycle (when ovulation begins), people may feel sluggish or forgetful, and symptoms from health problems like depression or irritable bowel syndrome may worsen.

qa.question: What causes mood swings in periods?
qa.answer: The most known speculation for mood swings are the fluctuation in levels of hormones or chemical messengers in the body, most typically estrogen and progesterone. The impact of these levels affect neurotransmitters in the brain, such as serotonin, which influences mood.

qa.question: Do all girls experience mood swings on their periods?
qa.answer: Not every girl experiences mood swings, but it is a common occurrence, a large global survey found that 64% of people who menstruate experience premenstrual mood swings or anxiety.

qa.question: What hormones cause mood swings?
qa.answer: Estrogen and progesterone.

qa.question: Can my period make my anxiety or stress worse?
qa.answer: Yes, symptoms for stress and anxiety typically worsen during a period because of a hormonal change during the menstrual cycle. Before a period begins, estradiol and progesterone decline rapidly and the draw back can underlie the symptoms.

qa.question: Is feeling anxious during my cycle normal?
qa.answer: It is different for everyone, most can feel more irritable, sad, or just experience general mood swings, but it does not directly mean you have anxiety.

qa.question: Is anxiety a part of PMS and PMDD?
qa.answer: Some with anxiety problems can experience a jump in their symptoms in premenstrual. There is a possible link between PMS and anxiety. But for a diagnosis of PMDD, you must have one or more of these symptoms: Mood swings, Depressed mood, Anxiety, Irritability.

qa.question: How do I know I should get help for my anxiety during my menstrual cycle?
qa.answer: First, look at your symptoms and how those symptoms affect your lifestyle. If it interferes with your ability to work or go to school and impair the way you socialize, you should seek help from a doctor.

qa.question: What can I do to stop feeling anxious during my period?
qa.answer: Exercise, Restorative Yoga, Avoiding Caffeine, Magnesium, Vitamin B6, L-theanine (typically found in green tea).

qa.question: Is it normal to cry more during my period?
qa.answer: Many women experience heightened emotions during their periods, so you are not alone! Your hormones are typically the reason why you feel more sad most of the time.

qa.question: Why do I cry more during my period?
qa.answer: The reason is not definitively known, but experts believe the cause of the sadness and heightened emotions are due to the drop in estrogen and progesterone levels.

qa.question: What are the causes for sadness during periods?
qa.answer: Low serotonin levels, Poor sleep quality, Changes in appetite, Not exercising, Stress.

qa.question: How do I stop crying and feel sad during my period?
qa.answer: The feeling often goes away, but if it doesn’t, it is best to consult with a doctor.

qa.question: How to deal with sadness during period
qa.answer: Eat food with high omega-3 fatty acids such as fish, Build exercise or activity into lifestyle, Avoid salty foods, Yoga, Cut out caffeine, Essential oils may help.

qa.question: Does PMS change with age?
qa.answer: Symptoms of PMS can worsen in ages 30-40. Usually, symptoms end when you reach menopause.

qa.question: Do mood swings end after your period ends?
qa.answer: Symptoms of periods may vary for people, but symptoms, such as mood swings, may linger for a few days even after a period ends, this is called Post-Menstrual Syndrome.

qa.question: What is Post-Menstrual Syndrome?
qa.answer: Post-Menstrual Syndrome is a term used to describe symptoms that are ongoing even after a period ends. It can be both physical and emotional.

qa.question: What causes mood swings to keep going even after a period ends?
qa.answer: One of the possibilities of on-going emotional symptoms even after a period ends could be hormonal imbalance.

qa.question: Does everyone experience symptoms even after a period ends?
qa.answer: Not everyone experiences prolonged symptoms before and after a period. Post-menstrual symptoms tend to be less common than Pre-menstrual ones. Up to 90% experience Pre-menstrual symptoms while up to 20-40 experiences Post-menstrual symptoms.

qa.question: What are the symptoms of PMS?
qa.answer: The symptoms range from physical to psychological ones, psychological being the most commonly reported which can be characterized through mood swings and anxiety. They may present themselves through irritability, anger, or sadness.

qa.question: How long do mood swings last in a period?
qa.answer: Symptoms usually begin 7-10 days before your period starts and continue for the first few days of your period. Though, there is a possibility you experience mood swings even after your period ends.

qa.question: Can stress affect my period?
qa.answer: Heightened stress can most likely worsen a period. Some effects stress may induce can be: Infrequent periods, Irregular periods, Heavier periods, Prolonged periods, On-going symptoms even after period ends.

qa.question: Why does stress affect my period?
qa.answer: Stress affects hormones in your body which may in turn affect other hormones. Due to periods and hormones being linked together, this can possibly affect your period, too. There is a possibility that stress leads to a decrease in production of reproductive hormones.

qa.question: How can I manage stress during my period?
qa.answer: Exercise, Sleep, Social engagement, Mindfulness, Magnesium, Relaxation exercises.

qa.question: Why am I so tired during my period?
qa.answer: Decrease in estrogen and progesterone hormones, Uncomfortable period symptoms which may directly impact your sleep, Mood swings.

qa.question: Can my period cause me to overthink more?
qa.answer: Yes, it is possible that you overthink more during your periods. This can be because of hormonal changes and symptoms like mood swings which directly influence your mood.

qa.question: Why do I feel ashamed about my period?
qa.answer: It’s okay to feel ashamed about your period! But it is always best to consult with your parents and doctor regarding your period. Menstruation is a completely normal thing, and you shouldn’t be afraid to speak up and have a conversation about it!

qa.question: What are emotional symptoms during periods?
qa.answer: Tension, Anxiousness, Crying, Trouble sleeping, Poor concentration, Social withdrawal.

qa.question: Why do I feel angry during my period?
qa.answer: Anger and irritability is one of the most common feelings during a period. A common cause for anger can be reduced serotonin levels.

qa.question: Can being angry on my period be a problem?
qa.answer: Feeling angry is a normal, human emotion. There is nothing wrong about being mad during your period, so as long as you are not hurting anyone, it is totally fine feeling angry!

qa.question: How can I help my anger during my period?
qa.answer: Track your emotions, Avoid triggers, Lifestyle changes, Manage your stress, Herbal Supplements, Diet changes.

qa.question: How do I apologize for my anger during my period?
qa.answer: Feeling angry is normal, but when it affects someone else, a conversation must be made. It is important to take responsibility for it through apologies. Communication is key for any relationship, so, by apologizing, you are taking accountability and acknowledging your wrongs.

qa.question: What can I do to be less tired during my period?
qa.answer: As much as sleep helps, there are numerous ways to help decrease tiredness during periods. Physical activity helps boost energy levels which, in turn, may help boost your mood and decrease tiredness. It is also good to keep track of your hydration levels and drink plenty of water!

qa.question: What makes me tired during my period?
qa.answer: Low estrogen, Higher energy demands, Anemia.
